items.title: Stock Market Today, Feb. 9: Kyndryl Share Price Halved After Earnings Miss and Delayed Filing
items.description: Today, Feb. 9, 2026, investors confront a rare mix of accounting review, leadership shake-up, and legal scrutiny at this IT services giant.

items.title: Kyndryl Sinks as CFO Departs, Accounting Review Disclosed
items.description: The stock fell a record 55% to close at its lowest level since December 2022 on news that Kyndryl Chief Financial Officer David Wyshner had left the position and that General Counsel Edward Sebold departed the post, effective immediately.  Kyndryl is reviewing its cash management practices, related disclosures including those regarding adjusted free cash flow, the efficacy of its internal control over financial reporting and certain other matters, according to a filing.  Kyndryl’s third quarter results missed expectations, and the company lowered its full-year forecast for both adjusted Ebitda and adjusted pretax profit.

items.title: Kyndryl Stock Drops 55%. It’s So Much Worse Than an Earnings Miss.
items.description: Kyndryl Holdings  stock plummeted on Monday after the company announced the immediate departures of chief financial officer and general counsel.  CFO David Wyshner and General Counsel Edward Sebold vacated their positions, the company said.  Kyndryl shares sank 55% to $10.59, the largest single-day percent decrease on record, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

items.title: Google Goes Long With 100-Year Bond Sale. We’ve Seen This Before.
items.description: Alphabet  the parent company of Google, is playing with both time and debt this week, with plans to raise US$20 billion amid a challenging week for the bond market as part of its larger effort to invest around US$185 billion this year, with more expected after that, on artificial intelligence technologies.  Google’s so-called “century bond” will join a relatively short list of corporate paper sold with a 100-year maturity, including a US$2.5 billion “green bond” offering from  Ford Motor Co  in 2021.  Motorola  which split into two companies in 2011, was the last high-grade company to issue a 100-year bond, selling the paper in 1997.
